title: Tumor Lysis Syndrome
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Oncology Fellows, Critical Care, Emergency Medicine
objectives: Define laboratory and clinical TLS using Cairo-Bishop criteria
Risk stratify patients for TLS prophylaxis
Manage electrolyte derangements in established TLS

guidelineReferences.name: Cairo-Bishop TLS Classification
guidelineReferences.citation: Cairo MS, Bishop M. Tumour lysis syndrome: new therapeutic strategies and classification. Br J Haematol. 2004;127:3-11

vignette.text:
A 45-year-old man with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma presents for initiation of chemotherapy. He has bulky abdominal disease with lymph nodes measuring up to 12 cm. Labs show LDH 3,200 U/L (markedly elevated), WBC 45,000/μL with circulating lymphoma cells, and baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL.

Past Medical History: Hypertension

Medications: Lisinopril

Allergies: NKDA

Planned Treatment: R-EPOCH chemotherapy starting tomorrow

physicalExam.abdomen: Distended with palpable large abdominal masses, hepatosplenomegaly
physicalExam.lymphatics: Bilateral bulky cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymphadenopathy

imaging.CT: Bulky retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymphadenopathy, largest mass 12 cm

questions.stem: This patient is about to receive chemotherapy for Burkitt lymphoma.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of tumor lysis syndrome?

options.text: Rapid tumor cell death releases intracellular contents: POTASSIUM (hyperkalemia → arrhythmias), PHOSPHORUS (hyperphosphatemia → binds calcium → hypocalcemia), NUCLEIC ACIDS (metabolized to URIC ACID → crystallizes in renal tubules → AKI); Occurs 12-72 hours after chemotherapy, or spontaneously in highly proliferative tumors
TLS is caused by chemotherapy toxicity only
TLS only affects the liver
Calcium levels increase in TLS

questions.explanation: TLS pathophysiology: Rapid tumor cell lysis releases intracellular contents: (1) POTASSIUM: Hyperkalemia → cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness; (2) PHOSPHORUS: Hyperphosphatemia → binds calcium → SECONDARY HYPOCALCEMIA → tetany, seizures, arrhythmias; (3) NUCLEIC ACIDS: Metabolized to hypoxanthine → xanthine → URIC ACID → precipitates in renal tubules → AKI (also calcium-phosphate crystals cause AKI). Timeline: Usually 12-72 hours after chemotherapy initiation. Can occur spontaneously in highly proliferative tumors (Burkitt, ALL).

whyChain.why: Why does this cascade cause kidney failure?
whyChain.answer: Two mechanisms of AKI: (1) Uric acid crystals precipitate in renal tubules (especially in acidic urine) → obstructive nephropathy; (2) Calcium-phosphate crystals deposit in renal parenchyma → nephrocalcinosis. AKI worsens all electrolyte abnormalities by reducing excretion, creating a dangerous spiral.

sources.text: StatPearls: Tumor Lysis Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to understand the diagnostic criteria for TLS.
questions.question: What are the Cairo-Bishop criteria for TLS diagnosis?

options.text: LABORATORY TLS: ≥2 of: Uric acid ≥8 mg/dL (or 25% increase), Potassium ≥6 mEq/L (or 25% increase), Phosphorus ≥4.5 mg/dL (or 25% increase), Calcium ≤7 mg/dL (or 25% decrease); CLINICAL TLS: Laboratory TLS PLUS ≥1 of: Creatinine ≥1.5x ULN, Cardiac arrhythmia/sudden death, Seizure
Only hyperuricemia is needed for diagnosis
Any single electrolyte abnormality defines TLS
Clinical TLS doesn't require lab abnormalities

questions.explanation: Cairo-Bishop Criteria: LABORATORY TLS: ≥2 abnormalities (within 3 days before or 7 days after chemotherapy): Uric acid ≥8 mg/dL or 25% increase from baseline, Potassium ≥6 mEq/L or 25% increase, Phosphorus ≥4.5 mg/dL (adults) or 25% increase, Calcium ≤7 mg/dL or 25% decrease. CLINICAL TLS: Laboratory TLS PLUS ≥1 clinical manifestation: Creatinine ≥1.5x upper limit of normal, Cardiac arrhythmia or sudden death, Seizure. Clinical TLS is more severe with higher mortality.

whyChain.answer: Currently has: Uric acid 9.2 (elevated, meets criterion), Potassium 4.8 (normal), Phosphorus 4.2 (normal), Calcium 9.0 (normal), Creatinine 1.4 (mildly elevated). Does NOT yet meet TLS criteria (only 1 abnormality). However, at VERY HIGH RISK once chemotherapy starts - needs aggressive prophylaxis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Tumor Lysis Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to assess this patient's TLS risk.
questions.question: How do you risk stratify patients for TLS?

options.text: All cancer patients are at equal TLS risk

HIGH RISK: Burkitt lymphoma, ALL (especially with WBC >100K), bulky disease (>10cm), high LDH (>2x ULN), high WBC, pre-existing renal dysfunction, elevated baseline uric acid, venetoclax initiation; INTERMEDIATE: Other aggressive lymphomas, AML with WBC 25-100K; LOW: Solid tumors, indolent lymphomas, CLL without bulky disease
Only hematologic malignancies cause TLS
Tumor size doesn't affect risk

questions.explanation: TLS Risk Stratification: HIGH RISK: Burkitt lymphoma (highest risk - this patient!), ALL with high WBC (>100K), Bulky disease (>10 cm), LDH >2x ULN, High proliferation rate, Pre-existing renal dysfunction, Elevated baseline uric acid, Venetoclax in CLL. INTERMEDIATE: Other aggressive NHL, AML with WBC 25-100K, CML blast crisis. LOW: Most solid tumors, Indolent lymphomas, CLL without bulky disease, Multiple myeloma. This patient: HIGH RISK (Burkitt + bulky disease + high LDH + elevated uric acid + renal dysfunction).

whyChain.answer: Burkitt lymphoma has extremely high proliferation rate (Ki-67 often >95%), high tumor burden, and is highly chemosensitive. This combination means massive, rapid cell death with chemotherapy. Can develop TLS within hours of treatment initiation.

questions.stem: You need to implement TLS prophylaxis for this high-risk patient.
questions.question: What are the prevention strategies for TLS?

options.text: Rasburicase should be given to all patients

HYDRATION: Aggressive IV fluids (2-3 L/m²/day) starting 24-48h before chemo, maintain UOP >100 mL/hr; LOW/INTERMEDIATE RISK: Allopurinol 100 mg/m² q8h (start 2-3 days before, continue 10-14 days); HIGH RISK or elevated uric acid: RASBURICASE (single 3-6 mg dose or 0.2 mg/kg); Monitor labs q4-8h in high-risk patients
Prophylaxis is not needed in high-risk patients
Limit fluids to prevent edema

questions.explanation: TLS Prevention: (1) HYDRATION: Aggressive IV fluids (2-3 L/m²/day, often with saline), start 24-48 hours before chemotherapy, maintain urine output >80-100 mL/hr, avoid potassium in fluids; (2) HYPOURICEMIC THERAPY: LOW/INTERMEDIATE RISK: Allopurinol 100 mg/m² PO q8h (max 800 mg/day), start 2-3 days before chemo, continue 10-14 days - prevents NEW uric acid formation but doesn't reduce existing levels. HIGH RISK: RASBURICASE - converts uric acid to allantoin (water-soluble), rapid reduction in existing uric acid, single dose often sufficient (3-6 mg fixed dose or 0.2 mg/kg); (3) MONITORING: High-risk: Labs q4-6h for first 24-72h.

whyChain.why: Why rasburicase over allopurinol for this patient?
whyChain.answer: This patient has: HIGH risk (Burkitt, bulky, high LDH) + already elevated uric acid (9.2). Allopurinol only prevents NEW uric acid formation - it won't reduce existing hyperuricemia. Rasburicase directly converts existing uric acid to allantoin, causing rapid reduction (often within 4 hours). Given his elevated baseline, rasburicase is strongly preferred.

sources.text: StatPearls: Tumor Lysis Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You order rasburicase for this patient.
questions.question: What are the important considerations with rasburicase?

options.text: Labs don't need special handling

CONTRAINDICATED in G6PD deficiency (causes hemolysis, methemoglobinemia); Lab handling: Blood samples must be kept on ICE (rasburicase continues to work ex vivo → falsely low uric acid); Single dose (3-6 mg) often sufficient; Do NOT give with allopurinol simultaneously (no added benefit); Much faster than allopurinol
Rasburicase can be given to anyone
Must be given daily for 7 days

questions.explanation: Rasburicase considerations: (1) CONTRAINDICATION: G6PD DEFICIENCY - causes severe hemolysis and methemoglobinemia (screen before use, especially in high-risk populations: African, Mediterranean, Asian descent); (2) LAB HANDLING: Blood samples MUST be placed on ice immediately and processed within 4 hours - rasburicase continues converting uric acid ex vivo → falsely low results; (3) DOSING: Single fixed dose (3-6 mg) usually sufficient - recheck in 4 hours, repeat if needed; (4) Do NOT combine with allopurinol (no added benefit, allopurinol substrate for xanthine oxidase is depleted); (5) COST: Expensive but cost-effective in high-risk patients.

sources.text: StatPearls: Tumor Lysis Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: 24 hours after chemotherapy, labs show K 6.8, Phos 7.2, Ca 6.5, Uric acid 4.2 (on rasburicase).
questions.question: How do you manage electrolyte derangements in TLS?

options.text: HYPERKALEMIA (most urgent): Cardiac monitor, calcium gluconate (membrane stabilization), insulin/glucose, kayexalate, consider dialysis if severe/refractory; HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA: Phosphate binders (sevelamer, aluminum hydroxide), avoid calcium-based binders (worsens calcium-phosphate precipitation); HYPOCALCEMIA: Treat only if SYMPTOMATIC (tetany, seizures, arrhythmia) - correcting asymptomatic hypocalcemia worsens calcium-phosphate precipitation
Hyperkalemia is not dangerous
Aggressively correct hypocalcemia first
Give IV calcium to all patients

questions.explanation: Electrolyte management in TLS: (1) HYPERKALEMIA (most immediately life-threatening): Cardiac monitoring, Calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization (does NOT lower K), Insulin + glucose, Beta-agonists, Kayexalate (slow), Dialysis if refractory or K >7; (2) HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA: Phosphate binders (sevelamer preferred), dietary restriction, hydration to promote excretion, dialysis if severe; (3) HYPOCALCEMIA: Treat ONLY IF SYMPTOMATIC - giving calcium when phosphorus is high causes calcium-phosphate precipitation in tissues (including kidneys). Asymptomatic hypocalcemia will correct as phosphorus normalizes; (4) HYPERURICEMIA: Already addressed with rasburicase in this patient.

whyChain.answer: Calcium × Phosphorus product: When both are elevated, calcium-phosphate crystals precipitate in tissues - kidney (worsens AKI), soft tissues, vessels. Treating asymptomatic hypocalcemia with IV calcium dramatically worsens this precipitation. The hypocalcemia is CAUSED BY hyperphosphatemia and will correct when phosphorus normalizes.

questions.stem: Despite aggressive management, creatinine rises to 4.5 and potassium is 7.2 with ECG changes.
questions.question: When is dialysis indicated in TLS?

options.text: Dialysis is never needed for TLS

Dialysis indications: Refractory hyperkalemia (especially with ECG changes), Severe/symptomatic hypocalcemia despite phosphorus control, Volume overload unresponsive to diuretics, Severe metabolic acidosis, Uremic symptoms, Anuria/severe oliguria; CRRT often preferred for hemodynamic stability and continuous electrolyte control
Only peritoneal dialysis is effective
Wait for complete renal failure before dialysis

questions.explanation: Dialysis indications in TLS: (1) HYPERKALEMIA refractory to medical management (especially with ECG changes - peaked T waves, widened QRS); (2) Volume overload unresponsive to diuretics; (3) Severe/symptomatic hypocalcemia despite phosphorus control; (4) Severe metabolic acidosis; (5) Uremic complications (encephalopathy, pericarditis); (6) Severe oliguria/anuria. Modality: CONTINUOUS RRT (CRRT) often preferred - better hemodynamic stability, continuous electrolyte control, better for ongoing tumor lysis. Intermittent HD may be needed for severe hyperkalemia. Early nephrology consultation essential.

questions.stem: A different patient is starting venetoclax for CLL.
questions.question: What special TLS considerations exist for venetoclax?

options.text: Venetoclax has HIGH TLS risk in CLL, especially with bulky lymphadenopathy or high lymphocyte count; Requires gradual dose ramp-up (20mg → 50 → 100 → 200 → 400mg weekly); Hospitalization for first dose escalation in high-risk patients; Tumor debulking strategies; Intensive monitoring at each dose level
Outpatient initiation for all patients
Venetoclax has no TLS risk
Start at full dose immediately

questions.explanation: Venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) TLS considerations: High TLS risk in CLL, especially with bulky lymphadenopathy (≥5 cm) or high absolute lymphocyte count (≥25,000/μL). Management: (1) RAMP-UP DOSING: Gradual dose escalation over 5 weeks (20 → 50 → 100 → 200 → 400 mg); (2) Risk assessment at each dose level; (3) HIGH-RISK: Hospitalization for initial doses, prophylactic rasburicase, intensive monitoring; (4) Tumor debulking with obinutuzumab or other agents before venetoclax may reduce risk; (5) Labs before and 6-8 hours after each new dose during ramp-up. Similar considerations for other highly active targeted therapies.

sources.text: StatPearls: Tumor Lysis Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Returning to the original Burkitt lymphoma patient.
questions.question: What is the complete TLS management approach for this patient?

options.text: Allopurinol alone is sufficient

Pre-chemo (24-48h): Aggressive IV hydration (2-3 L/m²/day), maintain UOP >100 mL/hr; RASBURICASE single dose (3-6 mg) given elevated baseline uric acid - recheck in 4 hours; Hold allopurinol (not needed with rasburicase); During chemo: Labs q4-6h (K, Phos, Ca, uric acid, Cr), cardiac monitoring, nephrology on standby; Treat hyperkalemia aggressively, phosphate binders PRN, avoid treating asymptomatic hypocalcemia; Dialysis if refractory
Start chemotherapy without prophylaxis
Monitor labs once daily only

questions.explanation: Complete TLS management for this HIGH-RISK patient: PRE-CHEMOTHERAPY (start 24-48h before): Aggressive IV hydration (2-3 L/m²/day, NS or D5W + NS), Target UOP >100 mL/hr, Foley catheter for accurate I/O, RASBURICASE 3-6 mg IV (single dose) - he has elevated baseline uric acid, Recheck uric acid in 4 hours (on ice!), Hold lisinopril (can worsen AKI), Avoid NSAIDs and contrast. DURING CHEMOTHERAPY: Labs (K, Phos, Ca, uric acid, Cr, LDH) q4-6h for first 72 hours, Continuous cardiac monitoring, ICU or step-down unit admission, Nephrology consultation on standby. TREATMENT of established TLS: Hyperkalemia - aggressive standard treatment, Hyperphosphatemia - sevelamer, Hypocalcemia - treat only if symptomatic, Dialysis if refractory electrolyte abnormalities, severe AKI, or volume overload.

sources.text: StatPearls: Tumor Lysis Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
